expanded_description: Practice problems designed to reinforce mass-to-mass stoichiometric calculations, building confidence in converting between reactant and product masses using balanced equations.

core_concept.principle: Mass-to-mass stoichiometry requires three conversion steps: mass → moles → moles → mass, using molar masses and mole ratios from balanced equations.
core_concept.explanation: These practice problems help you master the complete mass-to-mass calculation pathway. Each problem reinforces the relationship between measured masses, mole conversions, and balanced equation coefficients.

image.alt: Diagram showing mass-to-mass practice problem structure
image.caption: Practice problems build confidence in mass-to-mass stoichiometric calculations

sections.introduction: Start by practicing the first step of mass-to-mass calculations: converting between mass and moles. This foundational skill is essential before attempting complete stoichiometric calculations.

key_concept.definition: Mass-to-mole conversions use molar mass as the conversion factor: $\text{moles} = \dfrac{\text{mass (g)}}{\text{molar mass (g/mol)}}$
key_concept.context: Every mass-to-mass calculation begins and ends with mass-to-mole or mole-to-mass conversions.

classifications.value: Used to convert measured mass to moles for stoichiometric calculations
classifications.behavior: Essential for entering the mole-based stoichiometry pathway

examples.explanation: $\text{mol } \text{H}_2 = \dfrac{10.0 \text{ g}}{2.016 \text{ g/mol}} = 4.96 \text{ mol}$
$\text{mol NaCl} = \dfrac{25.0 \text{ g}}{58.44 \text{ g/mol}} = 0.428 \text{ mol}$

classifications.value: Used to convert calculated moles back to measurable mass

examples.explanation: $\text{mass } \text{H}_2\text{O} = 3.0 \text{ mol} \times 18.02 \text{ g/mol} = 54.1 \text{ g}$
$\text{mass } \text{CO}_2 = 0.50 \text{ mol} \times 44.01 \text{ g/mol} = 22.0 \text{ g}$

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 1.1: Mass to Moles
worked_examples.problem: Convert $15.0$ g of magnesium ($\text{Mg}$) to moles.

step_by_step.explanation: Divide and round to 3 significant figures

worked_examples.concept_applied: Mass to moles uses division by molar mass.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 1.2: Moles to Mass
worked_examples.problem: Convert $2.50$ moles of sodium chloride ($\text{NaCl}$) to grams.

step_by_step.explanation: Multiply and round to 3 significant figures

worked_examples.concept_applied: Moles to mass uses multiplication by molar mass.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 1.3: Mass to Moles for a Compound
worked_examples.problem: How many moles are in $50.0$ g of water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$)?

step_by_step.explanation: Divide and round to 3 significant figures

worked_examples.concept_applied: Compound molar mass must be calculated before converting.

sections.key_concept: Apply the complete three-step pathway: mass → moles → moles → mass using molar masses and mole ratios.

classifications.value: Calculate mass of product formed from given mass of reactant

examples.explanation: $\text{g } \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{mol } \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{mol } \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{g } \text{NH}_3$
$\text{g } \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{mol } \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{mol } \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{g } \text{H}_2\text{O}$

classifications.value: Calculate mass of one reactant needed for given mass of another
classifications.behavior: Essential for determining how much of each reactant to measure

examples.explanation: $\text{g } \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{mol } \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{mol } \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{g } \text{H}_2$
$\text{g } \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{mol } \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{mol } \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{g } \text{O}_2$

classifications.value: Calculate mass of reactant needed to produce given mass of product
classifications.behavior: Used when target product amount is known

examples.explanation: $\text{g } \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{mol } \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{mol } \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{g } \text{N}_2$
$\text{g } \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{mol } \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{mol } \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{g } \text{H}_2$

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 2.1: Reactant to Product Mass
worked_examples.problem: How many grams of $\text{NH}_3$ are produced from $10.0$ g of $\text{N}_2$? ($\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NH}_3$)

step_by_step.explanation: Use mole ratio from equation: $\dfrac{2 \text{ mol } \text{NH}_3}{1 \text{ mol } \text{N}_2}$

worked_examples.concept_applied: Complete mass-to-mass pathway: g → mol → mol → g

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 2.2: Reactant to Reactant Mass
worked_examples.problem: How many grams of $\text{O}_2$ are needed to react with $5.00$ g of $\text{H}_2$? ($2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Reactant-to-reactant mass conversion determines required amounts.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 2.3: Product to Reactant Mass
worked_examples.problem: What mass of $\text{H}_2$ is needed to produce $20.0$ g of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$? ($2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Working backwards from product to find required reactant mass.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 2.4: Continuous Dimensional Analysis
worked_examples.problem: Calculate grams of $\text{CO}_2$ from $25.0$ g of $\text{CH}_4$ in one continuous calculation. ($\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$)

step_by_step.explanation: Chain all conversion factors in one calculation
step_by_step.calculation: $? \text{ g } \text{CO}_2 = 25.0 \text{ g } \text{CH}_4 \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol } \text{CH}_4}{16.04 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol } \text{CO}_2}{1 \text{ mol } \text{CH}_4} \times \dfrac{44.01 \text{ g } \text{CO}_2}{1 \text{ mol } \text{CO}_2}$

step_by_step.explanation: g $\text{CH}_4$ cancel, mol $\text{CH}_4$ cancel, mol $\text{CO}_2$ cancel, leaving g $\text{CO}_2$
Multiply across numerator, divide by denominator

sections.introduction: These problems involve more complex equations with polyatomic ions, hydrates, or multiple products, requiring careful molar mass calculations.

key_concept.definition: Complex mass calculations follow the same pathway but require careful attention to molar mass calculations for compounds with parentheses, hydrates, or polyatomic ions.
key_concept.context: The stoichiometric pathway remains unchanged; only the molar mass calculation becomes more complex.

categories.description: Hydrocarbon + oxygen produces carbon dioxide and water
categories.detailed_mechanism: Mass calculations connect fuel mass to product masses and oxygen consumption

examples.explanation: Calculate mass of $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ from given mass of $\text{CH}_4$
Calculate mass of $\text{O}_2$ needed for given mass of $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$

categories.description: Two aqueous solutions react to form an insoluble solid precipitate
categories.detailed_mechanism: Mass calculations determine precipitate mass from reactant masses

examples.explanation: Calculate mass of AgCl precipitate from given mass of $\text{AgNO}_3$
Calculate mass of $\text{BaSO}_4$ from given mass of $\text{BaCl}_2$

categories.description: Single compound breaks down into multiple products
categories.detailed_mechanism: Mass calculations show product distribution from single reactant mass

examples.explanation: Calculate mass of $\text{O}_2$ produced from given mass of $\text{KClO}_3$
Calculate mass of CaO from given mass of $\text{CaCO}_3$

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 3.1: Combustion Mass Calculation
worked_examples.problem: How many grams of $\text{CO}_2$ are produced when $50.0$ g of propane ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$) burns completely? ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 3\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$)

step_by_step.calculation: $? = 50.0 \text{ g} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol}}{44.09 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{3 \text{ mol } \text{CO}_2}{1 \text{ mol } \text{C}_3\text{H}_8} \times \dfrac{44.01 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ mol}}$

step_by_step.explanation: Multiply and divide, round to 3 significant figures

worked_examples.concept_applied: Combustion calculations use the same mass-to-mass pathway.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 3.2: Precipitation Mass Calculation
worked_examples.problem: What mass of AgCl precipitate forms from $25.0$ g of $\text{AgNO}_3$? ($\text{AgNO}_3 + \text{NaCl} \rightarrow \text{AgCl} + \text{NaNO}_3$)

step_by_step.explanation: Mole ratio is 1:1 from balanced equation
step_by_step.calculation: $? \text{ g AgCl} = 25.0 \text{ g } \text{AgNO}_3 \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol}}{169.88 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol AgCl}}{1 \text{ mol } \text{AgNO}_3} \times \dfrac{143.32 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ mol}}$

worked_examples.concept_applied: Precipitation reactions use standard mass-to-mass calculations.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 3.3: Decomposition Mass Calculation
worked_examples.problem: What mass of $\text{O}_2$ is produced from decomposition of $100.0$ g of $\text{KClO}_3$? ($2\text{KClO}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{KCl} + 3\text{O}_2$)

step_by_step.calculation: $? \text{ g } \text{O}_2 = 100.0 \text{ g} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol}}{122.55 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{3 \text{ mol } \text{O}_2}{2 \text{ mol } \text{KClO}_3} \times \dfrac{32.00 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ mol}}$

step_by_step.explanation: Multiply and divide, round to 4 significant figures

worked_examples.concept_applied: Decomposition calculations connect single reactant to multiple products.

sections.key_concept: Identifying and correcting common mass calculation errors strengthens understanding and prevents mistakes in future calculations.

classifications.value: Using incorrect molar masses in calculations

examples.explanation: Oxygen gas is $\text{O}_2$ ($32.00$ g/mol), not O ($16.00$ g/mol)
Must count 2 H atoms: $2 \times 1.008 + 16.00 = 18.02$ g/mol

classifications.value: Skipping the mole step or using wrong conversion sequence

examples.process: Wrong: g A → g B directly
examples.explanation: Must use: g A → mol A → mol B → g B
Three conversion steps required for mass-to-mass

examples.process: Rounding intermediate moles to 2 sig figs
examples.explanation: Should keep 4+ digits during calculation, round final answer only

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 4.1: Identify the Molar Mass Error
worked_examples.problem: A student calculated that $10.0$ g of $\text{H}_2$ produces $90.0$ g of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$. The correct answer is $89.4$ g. What error did the student likely make?

step_by_step.explanation: Student's answer ($90.0$ g) is close to correct ($89.4$ g) but slightly off
Student likely used rounded molar masses: $\text{H}_2 = 2.0$ g/mol (instead of $2.016$), $\text{H}_2\text{O} = 18.0$ g/mol (instead of $18.02$)
Student used rounded molar masses instead of precise values
step_by_step.calculation: Error: insufficient precision in molar masses

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: used rounded molar masses ($2.0$ and $18.0$) instead of precise values ($2.016$ and $18.02$)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Molar mass precision affects final answer accuracy.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 4.2: Verify Mass Conservation
worked_examples.problem: Verify that $10.0$ g $\text{H}_2$ + $79.4$ g $\text{O}_2$ → $89.4$ g $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ satisfies conservation of mass. ($2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$)

step_by_step.explanation: Product mass should equal total reactant mass
Check that reactant mass equals product mass

worked_examples.final_answer: Mass is conserved: $89.4$ g reactants = $89.4$ g products
worked_examples.concept_applied: Conservation of mass verifies stoichiometric calculations.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 4.3: Identify the Pathway Error
worked_examples.problem: A student calculated that $20.0$ g $\text{CH}_4$ produces $20.0$ g $\text{CO}_2$. Identify the error. ($\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$)

step_by_step.explanation: Student assumed reactant mass = product mass for individual substances

step_by_step.calculation: $20.0 \text{ g } \text{CH}_4 \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol}}{16.04 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol } \text{CO}_2}{1 \text{ mol } \text{CH}_4} \times \dfrac{44.01 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ mol}} = 54.9 \text{ g } \text{CO}_2$

step_by_step.explanation: (1) Assumed individual mass conservation; (2) Skipped mole step; (3) Didn't use molar masses or mole ratio
Only TOTAL mass is conserved, not individual substance mass
step_by_step.calculation: Total: $20.0 \text{ g } \text{CH}_4 + 80.0 \text{ g } \text{O}_2 = 54.9 \text{ g } \text{CO}_2 + 45.1 \text{ g } \text{H}_2\text{O} = 100.0 \text{ g}$

worked_examples.final_answer: Correct: $54.9$ g $\text{CO}_2$. Error: assumed individual mass conservation, skipped stoichiometry steps
worked_examples.concept_applied: Individual substance masses change; only total mass is conserved.

sections.introduction: Test your understanding with these problems. Try solving them before looking at the solutions.

key_concept.definition: Self-check problems allow you to verify your mass calculation skills independently.
key_concept.context: Cover the solutions and attempt each problem, then check your work.

process_flow.description: Ensure the chemical equation is properly balanced
process_flow.mechanism: Count atoms of each element on both sides

process_flow.description: Determine molar mass for given and unknown substances
process_flow.mechanism: Sum atomic masses from periodic table

process_flow.description: Chain all conversion factors with proper unit cancellation
process_flow.mechanism: g given → mol given → mol unknown → g unknown

process_flow.description: Compute answer and check significant figures
process_flow.mechanism: Multiply/divide, round to correct sig figs, verify reasonableness
process_flow.result: Final answer with correct units and precision

worked_examples.title: Self-Check Problem 1
worked_examples.problem: How many grams of $\text{NaCl}$ form from $10.0$ g of Na? ($2\text{Na} + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl}$)

step_by_step.explanation: g Na → mol Na → mol NaCl → g NaCl
step_by_step.calculation: $? = 10.0 \text{ g} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol}}{22.99 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{2 \text{ mol NaCl}}{2 \text{ mol Na}} \times \dfrac{58.44 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ mol}}$

worked_examples.title: Self-Check Problem 2
worked_examples.problem: What mass of $\text{O}_2$ is needed to burn $15.0$ g of $\text{Mg}$? ($2\text{Mg} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{MgO}$)

step_by_step.explanation: g Mg → mol Mg → mol $\text{O}_2$ → g $\text{O}_2$
step_by_step.calculation: $? = 15.0 \text{ g} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol}}{24.31 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol } \text{O}_2}{2 \text{ mol Mg}} \times \dfrac{32.00 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ mol}}$

step_by_step.explanation: Multiply and divide, round to 3 significant figures

worked_examples.title: Self-Check Problem 3
worked_examples.problem: How many grams of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ form from $5.00$ g of $\text{O}_2$? ($2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$)

step_by_step.calculation: $? = 5.00 \text{ g} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol}}{32.00 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{2 \text{ mol } \text{H}_2\text{O}}{1 \text{ mol } \text{O}_2} \times \dfrac{18.02 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ mol}}$

worked_examples.concept_applied: Product mass from reactant mass calculation.

worked_examples.title: Self-Check Problem 4
worked_examples.problem: What mass of $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ forms from $20.0$ g of Fe? ($4\text{Fe} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$)

step_by_step.explanation: g Fe → mol Fe → mol $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ → g $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$
step_by_step.calculation: $? = 20.0 \text{ g} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ mol}}{55.85 \text{ g}} \times \dfrac{2 \text{ mol } \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3}{4 \text{ mol Fe}} \times \dfrac{159.70 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ mol}}$

step_by_step.explanation: Multiply and divide, round to 3 significant figures

sections.key_concept: Learning from common errors helps you avoid them in your own work.

classifications.value: Performing calculations without verifying equation balance
classifications.behavior: Always count atoms on both sides before calculating

examples.explanation: Take 30 seconds to verify atom counts before calculating

classifications.value: Using atomic mass instead of molecular/compound molar mass

examples.explanation: Oxygen gas is $\text{O}_2$ ($32.00$ g/mol), not O ($16.00$ g/mol)
Hydrogen gas is $\text{H}_2$ ($2.016$ g/mol), not H ($1.008$ g/mol)

classifications.value: Rounding intermediate values before final answer
classifications.behavior: Keep extra digits during calculation; round only final answer

examples.process: Rounding moles to 2 sig figs mid-calculation
examples.explanation: Should keep 4+ digits during calculation, round final answer only

classifications.value: Accepting first calculated answer without checking
classifications.behavior: Always verify using conservation of mass and reasonableness checks

examples.explanation: Check units, significant figures, reasonableness, and mass balance

worked_examples.title: Mistake Analysis: Diatomic Element Error
worked_examples.problem: A student used $16.00$ g/mol for oxygen in a calculation involving $\text{O}_2$ gas. Explain the error and its impact.

step_by_step.explanation: Student used atomic mass of O instead of molar mass of $\text{O}_2$
Using half the correct molar mass doubles the calculated moles
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{mol} = \dfrac{\text{mass}}{\text{molar mass}}$; half the denominator = double the result

step_by_step.explanation: Oxygen gas exists as diatomic molecules ($\text{O}_2$)

step_by_step.calculation: Remember: H, N, O, F, Cl, Br, I (HONClBrI)

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: used atomic O mass instead of molecular $\text{O}_2$ mass; answer would be off by factor of 2
worked_examples.concept_applied: Diatomic elements must use molecular molar mass, not atomic mass.

worked_examples.title: Verification Checklist for Mass Calculations
worked_examples.problem: Outline a systematic verification approach for all mass-to-mass problems.

step_by_step.explanation: Verify atom counts match on both sides

step_by_step.calculation: Sum atomic masses with correct subscripts

step_by_step.explanation: Verify all units cancel except desired final unit
Confirm answer has correct number of sig figs
Verify total reactant mass = total product mass

worked_examples.final_answer: Follow the 6-check verification checklist for all mass calculations
worked_examples.concept_applied: Systematic verification catches errors before finalizing answers.

summary: Mass calculation practice problems reinforce stoichiometric skills through progressive difficulty levels: (1) Basic mass-to-mole conversions, (2) Complete mass-to-mass calculations using three-step pathway, (3) Complex calculations with combustion, precipitation, and decomposition reactions, (4) Error analysis and verification. Key principles: always balance equations first, calculate accurate molar masses, use correct conversion pathway (g → mol → mol → g), maintain proper significant figures, and verify answers using conservation of mass. Common errors include using unbalanced equations, incorrect molar masses (especially for diatomic elements), rounding intermediate values, and failing to verify results. Self-check problems allow independent skill verification. A systematic 6-check verification checklist ensures accuracy: equation balance, molar mass accuracy, unit cancellation, significant figures, reasonableness, and mass conservation. Regular practice with varied problem types builds confidence and prevents common mistakes in quantitative chemistry. Mastery of mass-to-mass calculations is essential for all stoichiometric applications including limiting reactant problems, percent yield calculations, and laboratory work.
